claim: AGENTS.md is positioned as a predictable README-like place for coding-agent context and project instructions, not a replacement for human README files.
quote: Think of AGENTS.md as a README for agents: a dedicated, predictable place to provide the context and instructions to help AI coding agents work on your project.

claim: AGENTS.md examples include setup commands, code style, testing instructions, and PR instructions; this supports using it as an agent harness entrypoint.
quote: Example sections include Setup commands, Code style, Testing instructions, and PR instructions.

claim: AGENTS.md instructions should be living documentation and closer files win when instructions conflict.
quote: The closest AGENTS.md to the edited file wins; explicit user chat prompts override everything. Treat AGENTS.md as living documentation.

claim: Claude Code treats CLAUDE.md files as persistent instructions loaded into context; they are context, not enforced configuration, so concise structured instructions improve adherence.
quote: CLAUDE.md files are loaded into the context window at the start of every session... Because they’re context rather than enforced configuration, how you write instructions affects how reliably Claude follows them. Specific, concise, well-structured instructions work best.

claim: Claude recommends adding durable instructions when the assistant repeats a mistake, code review catches something it should know, or a new teammate would need the same context.
quote: Add to it when: Claude makes the same mistake a second time... A code review catches something Claude should have known... A new teammate would need the same context to be productive.

claim: Skills are configured with YAML frontmatter and markdown content; they can encode reusable domain knowledge, conventions, patterns, and workflows.
quote: Skills are configured through YAML frontmatter at the top of SKILL.md and the markdown content that follows. Skill files can contain any instructions... Reference content adds knowledge... Conventions, patterns, style guides, domain knowledge.

claim: Skills can restrict activation by paths and pre-approve allowed tools, making them suitable as scoped behavior modules rather than global prompts.
quote: paths... Glob patterns that limit when this skill is activated... allowed-tools... Tools Claude can use without asking permission when this skill is active.

claim: Subagents run with their own context window, custom system prompt, tool access, and independent permissions; they are useful when repeated side tasks would flood the main conversation.
quote: Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions.

claim: Skills can be preloaded into subagents, but tool access and Skill availability are still controlled separately.
quote: The full content of each listed skill is injected into the subagent’s context at startup... To prevent a subagent from invoking skills entirely, omit Skill from the tools list or add it to disallowedTools.

claim: Hooks execute at defined lifecycle events such as PreToolUse, PostToolUse, InstructionsLoaded, ConfigChange and FileChanged, giving a deterministic layer around model behavior.
quote: Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session... InstructionsLoaded... ConfigChange... FileChanged... PreToolUse... PostToolUse.

claim: PreToolUse hooks can block unsafe operations and PostToolUse hooks can run checks such as linters after edits.
quote: This example validates Bash commands with the PreToolUse hook and runs a linter after file edits with PostToolUse.

claim: Anthropic recommends the simplest viable agent design, transparent planning, and thorough tool documentation/testing; complexity should be added only when it improves outcomes.
quote: Start with simple prompts, optimize them with comprehensive evaluation, and add multi-step agentic systems only when simpler solutions fall short... Carefully craft your agent-computer interface (ACI) through thorough tool documentation and testing.

claim: Coding agents are valuable when output quality can be objectively measured, but human review remains crucial beyond automated tests.
quote: Output quality can be measured objectively... automated testing helps verify functionality, human review remains crucial for ensuring solutions align with broader system requirements.

claim: MCP standardizes connecting AI apps to external systems such as data sources, tools, and workflows; harness design must govern these connections.
quote: MCP is an open-source standard for connecting AI applications to external systems... data sources... tools... workflows.

claim: OWASP LLM risks directly map to agent harness concerns: prompt injection, insecure output handling, supply chain vulnerabilities, sensitive information disclosure, insecure plugin design, excessive agency, and overreliance.
quote: LLM01: Prompt Injection... LLM02: Insecure Output Handling... LLM05: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities... LLM08: Excessive Agency... LLM09: Overreliance.

claim: Promptfoo positions evals as testing prompts, models, and RAG pipelines; this supports treating prompt/context/harness changes as testable artifacts.
quote: Evaluations: Test and evaluate your prompts, models, and RAG pipelines.

claim: SWE-bench frames coding-agent evaluation as resolving real-world GitHub issues and notes containerized evaluation for reproducibility.
quote: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-world Github Issues?... moving to a fully containerized evaluation harness using Docker for more reproducible evaluations.

claim: Windsurf also separates persistent context into memories, rules, workflows, skills and AGENTS.md; this confirms the ecosystem pattern of multiple context layers that can drift.
quote: Memories, Rules, Workflows, or Skills?... Cascade Memories & Rules persist context across Cascade conversations with auto-generated memories and user-defined rules.

claim: GitHub Copilot supports repository custom instructions, showing that repo-level agent guidance is now a cross-tool pattern, not just AGENTS.md.
quote: Repository custom instructions for GitHub Copilot let teams customize Copilot responses for a repository.

claim: GitHub warns against conflicting custom instruction sets; this supports the article’s alignment-drift framing.
quote: If you are concerned about response quality, you can temporarily disable repository instructions... try to avoid providing conflicting sets of instructions.

claim: Claude Code best practices recommend giving Claude a way to verify work and including Bash commands, code style, and workflow rules in CLAUDE.md.
quote: Give Claude a way to verify its work... CLAUDE.md is a special file that Claude reads at the start of every conversation. Include Bash commands, code style, and workflow rules.

claim: Claude Code best practices recommend explore-first, plan-then-code workflows and active course correction; this maps to harness instructions and eval cases.
quote: Explore first, then plan, then code... Course-correct early and often... Manage context aggressively.

claim: Claude Code permissions provide fine-grained control over what the agent can access and do, including tool-specific permission rules.
quote: Control what Claude Code can access and do with fine-grained permission rules, modes, and managed policies.

claim: Additional directories may grant file access without loading configuration by default; this is a concrete example of context/tool scope being different from configuration scope.
quote: Additional directories grant file access, not configuration.

claim: Windsurf has a SKILL.md file format, confirming that skills as reusable workflow/context modules are an ecosystem pattern.
quote: Cascade Skills... Manual Creation... SKILL.md File Format... Required Frontmatter Fields.

claim: Windsurf workflows are another explicit workflow layer, reinforcing the need to align AGENTS.md, rules, skills, and workflows.
quote: Cascade Workflows... How it works... Workflow Storage Locations... System-Level Workflows.

claim: Gemini CLI uses hierarchical memory loaded from GEMINI.md files and can inspect/reload that instructional context.
quote: /memory: Manage the AI’s instructional context (hierarchical memory loaded from GEMINI.md files)... show... refresh.

claim: Aider supports convention files read into chat, showing another implementation of project-specific coding-agent guidance.
quote: Specifying coding conventions... read: [CONVENTIONS.md, anotherfile.txt].

claim: OpenCode agents are specialized assistants configured for specific tasks/workflows with custom prompts, models, and tool access.
quote: Agents are specialized AI assistants that can be configured for specific tasks and workflows... custom prompts, models, and tool access.

claim: OpenCode distinguishes primary agents and subagents; the plan agent can analyze/review without code changes, a useful pattern for safe review skills.
quote: Use the plan agent to analyze code and review suggestions without making any code changes.

claim: Promptfoo includes agent trajectory assertions such as tool-used, tool-args-match, tool-sequence and step-count; this directly supports harness evals for agent behavior, not only final text.
quote: trajectory:tool-used... trajectory:tool-args-match... trajectory:tool-sequence... trajectory:step-count.

claim: Promptfoo red teaming frames AI apps as nondeterministic and security-sensitive; this supports testing adversarial harness failures like prompt injection and excessive agency.
quote: Generative features with unpredictable results... Non-deterministic ML models introduce uncertainty & chaotic behavior.

claim: NIST AI RMF provides a risk-management frame for AI systems; for coding-agent harnesses this supports risk-tiered permissions/evals.
quote: AI Risk Management Framework.

claim: GitHub’s Copilot guidance emphasizes providing context such as workspace awareness and selected code; context engineering is a practical developer action.
quote: Use the @workspace agent... aware of your entire workspace... Highlight relevant code.
